parentQuestion: During dark adaptation, rhodopsin accumulates in the rods.

scaffolds.q: Rods are the photoreceptors for dim light (night) vision.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Rods are extremely sensitive but only detect brightness, not color.

scaffolds.q: Rhodopsin is the photopigment that makes rods light-sensitive.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Without rhodopsin, rods can't detect photons.

scaffolds.q: In bright light, rhodopsin molecules are continuously bleached.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Light breaks rhodopsin into opsin + all-trans-retinal.

scaffolds.q: In darkness, no light = no bleaching (rhodopsin is preserved).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The destruction process stops in the dark.

scaffolds.q: Meanwhile, the regeneration process keeps running (rebuilding rhodopsin).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The RPE keeps converting retinal back to the active 11-cis form.

scaffolds.q: Net result in darkness: Rhodopsin ACCUMULATES (regeneration > bleaching).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This is dark adaptation at the molecular level.

scaffolds.q: Fully dark-adapted rods contain ~10^8 rhodopsin molecules each.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! This enormous supply enables single-photon detection.

scaffolds.q: Dark adaptation = rhodopsin builds up in rods = increased sensitivity.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! More rhodopsin molecules means higher probability of catching photons.

scaffolds.q: The answer is TRUE - during dark adaptation, rhodopsin accumulates in rods.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! This is the molecular mechanism behind improving night vision over 20-40 minutes in darkness.
